mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 116i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 116i engine typically costs £1,500–£3,000 supply only depending on generation, with used units available from £750–£1,800. Fitting adds £800–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends entirely on which generation. The early N45 and N43 are simple but suffer from timing chain issues. The N13 turbo has a reputation for timing chain and oil consumption problems. The B38 three-cylinder (2015 onwards) is genuinely reliable. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 116i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear on N45, N43 and N13 engines, injector issues on N43 and N13, oil consumption on N13 and B38, and cooling system wear on B38. The B38 is significantly more reliable than its predecessors.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 116i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept £5,000–£8,000 F20 116i, a £2,000 recon is 25–40% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired £2,000 E87 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 116i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: 2004–2007 is N45B16, 2007–2011 is N43B16, 2011–2015 is N13B16, 2015 onwards is B38A15. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 116i timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement (£600–£1,800) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£1,200–£3,000 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold-start rattle on any pre-2015 116i, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (N45, N43, N13)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 1–5 seconds, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors
problems.replacementCost: £1,200–£3,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any cold-start rattle on these engines requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N45, N43 and N13 engines share a well-documented timing chain weakness. The chain guides are plastic and can break, sending debris into the oil pickup. The N13 is particularly notorious. BMW did not formally recognise the issue in the UK/EU, making preventative replacement essential. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. A £600–£1,800 preventative chain replacement is dramatically cheaper than a £1,200+ engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Direct Injection and Injector Issues (N43, N13)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, vibration, hesitation on acceleration, misfire under load, engine warning light
problems.recommendation: The N43's direct injection system is sensitive to fuel quality and injector condition. Injector failure causes vibration and rough running. The N13, being a direct-injection turbo engine, suffers similar issues. Use high-quality fuel and have injectors checked at around 80,000 miles. Catching a failing injector early prevents secondary damage to the catalytic converter.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector failure guide

problems.title: Oil Consumption and PCV Issues (N13, B38)
problems.symptoms: Oil level dropping between services, blue smoke on startup, rough idle
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 (PCV valve replacement or oil separator service)
problems.recommendation: The N13 is known for oil consumption, often caused by a failing PCV system or worn piston rings. The B38 can also suffer from excessive oil consumption. Check oil levels regularly — if you're topping up more than 1L between services, have the PCV system inspected. Ignoring oil consumption can lead to carbon build-up and, eventually, engine damage.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 116i? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2015 B38-engined car — the most reliable 116i generation by some margin
* N45/N43/N13 with documented timing chain work — evidence of the upgrade is essential
* Full service history with correct BMW-spec oil (5W-30 LL-01 or equivalent)
* Evidence of regular maintenance and quality fuel (especially important for N43/N13)
avoidIf: * Pre-2015 N45/N43/N13 with no proof of timing chain work — you're buying a known risk
* Any cold-start rattle — this is almost certainly timing chain tensioner wear
* Evidence of oil consumption or rising oil levels — indicates PCV or ring issues
* Poor service history — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 116i badge spans five very different engines. The early N45 and N43 are simple but aging — timing chain work is the defining question. The N13 turbo is quick but has a reputation for timing chain and oil consumption issues. The B38 three-cylinder is the pick of the bunch — modern, efficient, and genuinely reliable. Buy on history, not on price alone.

cta.label: → Compare 116i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 116i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattles lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain warning on N45/N43/N13)
2. Service History — including which engine era (N45, N43, N13, or B38) and oil change intervals
3. Oil Level — Check for rising oil level (diesel in oil — not applicable to petrol) or excessive consumption
4. Idle Quality — Any vibration or roughness? (injector or PCV issues on N43/N13)
5. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
6. Coolant Level — Any loss? (cooling system wear on B38)
7. Turbo Performance (N13/B38) — Test acceleration; any hesitation or whistling?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
